stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density \( f(x) = [[c]]\,x^{[[m]]} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) elsewhere). What is the expected value \( E[X] \)?
distractor_rationales: This assumes \( X \) is uniform on \( [0, [[b]]] \) with mean \( [[b]]/2 \), ignoring the density.
This forgets the factor of [[b]] from the interval length.
This equals \( [[b]] \); it uses \( [[m]]+1 \) in the denominator instead of \( [[m]]+2 \).
This uses \( [[m]]+3 \) in the denominator instead of \( [[m]]+2 \).
This uses \( [[m]] \) and \( [[m]]+1 \) instead of \( [[m]]+1 \) and \( [[m]]+2 \).
This swaps the numerator factor to \( [[m]]+2 \) and denominator to \( [[m]]+1 \).

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density \( f(x) = [[c]]\,x^{[[m]]} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) elsewhere). What is the variance \( \operatorname{Var}(X) \)?
solution: Use \( \operatorname{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - E[X]^2 \) with \( E[X^2] = \frac{([[m]]+1)[[b]]^2}{[[m]]+3} \) and \( E[X] = \frac{([[m]]+1)[[b]]}{[[m]]+2} \), which simplifies to \( \operatorname{Var}(X) = \dfrac{([[m]]+1)[[b]]^2}{([[m]]+2)^2([[m]]+3)} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the variance formula for a UNIFORM density, ignoring the actual power-law shape.
This is \( E[X^2] \) alone, forgetting to subtract \( E[X]^2 \).
This is \( E[X]^2 \) alone, forgetting to subtract it FROM \( E[X^2] \) rather than reporting it directly.
This drops one factor of \( ([[m]]+2) \) from the denominator.
This is \( E[X] \), the mean, not the variance.
This swaps the roles of \( ([[m]]+1) \) and \( ([[m]]+3) \) in the formula.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density \( f(x) = [[c]]([[L]] - x) \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[L]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) elsewhere), so outcomes near \( 0 \) are more likely than outcomes near [[L]]. What is the expected value \( E[X] \)?
distractor_rationales: This assumes \( X \) is uniform on \( [0,[[L]]] \) with mean \( [[L]]/2 \), ignoring that the density decreases.
This is the mean of the MIRROR density (more likely near [[L]]), not this decreasing one.
This is off by a factor of \( 3/4 \) from the correct mean.
This is half the correct mean.
This is the wrong mean for a density that increases rather than decreases.
This is close to the uniform mean but shifted down by one, not derived from the actual density.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density \( f(x) = [[c]]([[L]] - x) \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[L]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) elsewhere). What is the variance \( \operatorname{Var}(X) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the variance formula for a UNIFORM density, ignoring the actual linear shape.
This drops a factor from the denominator (uses \( 9 \) instead of \( 18 \)).
This drops a factor from the denominator (uses \( 6 \) instead of \( 18 \)).
This uses too large a denominator, off by a factor of 2.
This is \( E[X] \), the mean, not the variance.
This uses too large a denominator, off by a factor of \( 4/3 \).
